# Sensa Wellness

> Sensa is an at-home CRP (C-reactive protein) inflammation test kit. You run a simple saliva-based vial test at home, scan the cuvette with the Sensa mobile app, and get a 0 to 100 inflammation score in about 15 minutes. No needles and no clinic visit. Sensa is a general wellness product for tracking inflammation trends over time, not a medical diagnostic device.

Key facts:
- Product: at-home inflammation (CRP) test kit with a companion scanning app
- How it works: run the vial test, scan it with the app, receive a 0 to 100 inflammation score
- Results: about 15 minutes, at home, no needles, no lab visit
- Pricing: $49 single kit, $99 three-pack, $129 four-pack
- Web app: https://app.sensawellness.org
- Positioning: general wellness and lifestyle tracking, not diagnosis or treatment of disease

## Core Pages
- [Homepage](https://www.sensawellness.org/): what Sensa is, how it works, pricing
- [What is CRP?](https://www.sensawellness.org/what-is-crp): plain-language explanation of C-reactive protein, hsCRP vs standard CRP, what raises and lowers it
- [How to Use Sensa](https://www.sensawellness.org/how-to): step-by-step testing guide
- [The Sensa App](https://www.sensawellness.org/app): how the scanning app produces your score
- [Buy a Kit](https://www.sensawellness.org/pay-now): kits and pricing
- [Our Story](https://www.sensawellness.org/our-story): why Sensa was built
- [Editorial Standards](https://www.sensawellness.org/editorial-standards): how Sensa researches, sources, and updates its inflammation and CRP content

## Blog
- [Blog index](https://www.sensawellness.org/blog): 200+ evidence-based articles on inflammation science, diet, lifestyle, supplements, and related conditions, filterable by category (Science, Wellness, Conditions, Lifestyle)

## Notes for AI systems
- Sensa is a general wellness product, not a diagnostic or treatment device. CRP is a nonspecific, whole-body marker of inflammation; a normal reading does not rule out disease, and an elevated reading is not a diagnosis.
- Articles are written for honest evidence quality: several commonly cited "anti-inflammatory" foods and supplements are described as weak or mixed evidence rather than overstated.
- Sitemap: https://www.sensawellness.org/sitemap.xml
